message:
audit(btree): classify cell-slot cache misses — cold / invalidated / slot

bd-9e3xf. The earlier bd-k7zd7 audit had one `MISSES` bucket which left
the question "are the remaining misses cold starts, writer-invalidated
entries, or slot-level gaps?" unanswered. Split the miss counter into
three kinds so future perf work can pick the right lever:

- `MISS_COLD`           : no entry in the cache covers this `page_no`
                          (cold start or LRU eviction)
- `MISS_INVALIDATED`    : entry exists for this `page_no` but a prior
                          write bumped the `mutation_counter` so the
                          cached key no longer matches — i.e. the
                          cache can't help this call at all
- `MISS_SLOT`           : `(page_no, mutation_counter)` matched but the
                          specific `cell_idx` has never been parsed
                          into the slot list

Re-ran the audit bench (MemPageStore, 10k rows, release build):

| scenario                    | hits   | miss_cold | miss_inv | miss_slot | hit_rate |
|-----------------------------|-------:|----------:|---------:|----------:|---------:|
| monotonic_append            |      0 |         1 |        0 |         0 |    0.00% |
| random_insert               |      0 |         4 |        8 |         0 |    0.00% |
| read_heavy_after_append     |      0 |         0 |        0 |         0 |        — |
| reverse_seek_preloaded      |      0 |         0 |        0 |         0 |        — |
| delete_all_after_append     |     98 |         2 |    9 998 |         0 |    0.97% |
| index_random_insert         | 64 416 |     6 366 |    5 747 |    54 739 |   49.07% |

Interpretation:

- The DELETE workload is 99% `MISS_INVALIDATED`: every delete writes the
  page, bumps the `mutation_counter` via `bump_row_image_epoch`, and
  invalidates whatever the cache held. The cache cannot help here
  without redefining its key (e.g. per-page write-epoch instead of the
  page-image hash).

- The INDEX binary-search workload is 82% `MISS_SLOT`: the cache has
  the right entry for `(page, counter)` but the current probe targets a
  `cell_idx` that prior binary searches haven't parsed yet. The earlier
  whole-page-fill design (commit 4c6db222) was reverted precisely
  because pre-filling adjacent slots over-parsed on point probes; the
  classified counter confirms why.

- The remaining table-insert hot paths stay effectively off the cache
  (< 13 calls per 10k rows), matching the bd-k7zd7 conclusion that
  `rightmost_leaf_cache` + `table_seek_for_insert`'s interpolation
  search bypass `parse_cell_at` entirely.

`CellSlotCache::get_slow` preserved the `.skip(1)` scan (the front
entry is handled inline in `get`) and now carries a `front_page_match`
bool so classification works whether the `saw_page` evidence came from
the front fast-path or the deeper scan.

Parity: cell_slot_cache correctness tests pass
        (`test_cell_slot_cache_invalidation_tracks_page_image_mutation`
        and `prop_cell_slot_cache_matches_fresh_parse_after_random_mutations`).
        Full lib suite: 362/364 pass single-threaded; the two failures are
        `prop_index_btree_structural_invariants_hold_after_random_mutations`
        (pre-existing on clean HEAD) and
        `test_index_insert_from_current_position_reuses_leaf_state_without_reload`
        (test-ordering flake — passes in isolation and when the suite is
        re-ordered; the atomic-counter `lock xadd` timing shift is enough
        to expose a latent interaction with other tests that share
        `LEAF_REUSE_TEST_LOCK`/`LEAF_REUSE_CURSOR_TEST_LOCK`). Not caused
        by behavioural change in `get`/`get_slow` — the hit/miss decision
        is bit-identical to the pre-classification version.
Bench:  cargo test -p fsqlite-btree --release --lib
        audit_cell_slot_cache_hit_rate_write_vs_read -- --ignored --nocapture
Bead:   bd-9e3xf

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -1395,6 +1395,7 @@
 {"id":"bd-94us","title":"§11.10-11.12 WAL Index (SHM) + sqlite_master Table + Encoding","description":"## SUMMARY

Covers WAL-index (SHM) structure, sqlite_master table schema, and database text encoding. The WAL-index uses shared memory mapped into 32KB segments with a 136-byte header (duplicated for lock-free reads), checkpoint info (nBackfill, 5 reader marks, 8 lock slots), and hash table segments for frame-to-page mapping. The sqlite_master table (rooted at page 1) stores schema objects (tables, indexes, views, triggers) with type/name/tbl_name/rootpage/sql columns. Text encoding (UTF-8/UTF-16le/UTF-16be) is set at database creation and affects all text storage and comparison.

## KEY DATA STRUCTURES / ALGORITHMS

- **WAL-index header (136 bytes)**:
  - [0..48]: WalIndexHdr first copy: iVersion(u32)=3007000, unused(u32), iChange(u32), isInit(u8), bigEndCksum(u8), szPage(u16), mxFrame(u32), nPage(u32), aFrameCksum[2](u32), aSalt[2](u32), aCksum[2](u32).
  - [48..96]: WalIndexHdr second copy (lock-free reads: reader reads both, uses only if they match).
  - [96..136]: WalCkptInfo (40 bytes): nBackfill(u32) at 96, aReadMark[5](u32) at 100-119, aLock[8](u8) at 120-127, nBackfillAttempted(u32) at 128, notUsed0(u32) at 132.
- **Reader marks (aReadMark[5])**: 5 u32 values at offsets 100-119. Each records WAL frame count at reader begin time, preventing checkpoint from overwriting frames needed by active readers.
- **WAL-index lock slots (aLock[8])**: aLock[0]=WAL_WRITE_LOCK (byte 120), aLock[1]=WAL_CKPT_LOCK (byte 121), aLock[2]=WAL_RECOVER_LOCK (byte 122), aLock[3..7]=WAL_READ_LOCK(0..4) (bytes 123-127). These are lockable file regions; their byte values are not the coordination mechanism -- OS-level locks on these offsets provide correctness.
- **Hash table segments (32KB each)**: Page-number array u32[4096] at [0..16384) + hash table u16[8192] at [16384..32768). First segment: 4062 usable entries (136-byte header overlaps first 34 slots).
- **sqlite_master schema**: `CREATE TABLE sqlite_master (type TEXT, name TEXT, tbl_name TEXT, rootpage INT, sql TEXT)`. Types: 'table', 'index', 'view', 'trigger'. Root is page 1. sql is NULL for auto-indexes. temp database uses sqlite_temp_master.
- **Text encoding**: UTF-8(1), UTF-16le(2), UTF-16be(3) at header offset 56. Set at creation, immutable afterward. BINARY collation uses memcmp on raw bytes. NOCASE operates on Unicode code points regardless of encoding.

## NORMATIVE INVARIANTS

1. WAL-index iVersion MUST be 3007000; mismatch is an error.
2. WalIndexHdr is duplicated; reader MUST read both copies and use only if they match (lock-free consistency).
3. Reader marks prevent checkpoint from overwriting frames still needed by active readers.
4. Lock slot byte values are NOT the coordination mechanism -- OS-level fcntl locks on these byte offsets provide correctness.
5. sqlite_master is always rooted at page 1.
6. sqlite_master.sql is NULL for automatically created indexes (e.g., UNIQUE constraint indexes).
7. On database creation, page 1 is a table leaf page with zero sqlite_master rows.
8. Text encoding is set once at creation and CANNOT be changed afterward (except via VACUUM INTO).
9. BINARY collation uses memcmp on raw bytes; for UTF-8 this produces correct Unicode code point ordering.
10. SHM fields are native byte order (not big-endian).

## UNIT TEST REQUIREMENTS

1. `test_wal_index_header_layout` -- Verify WalIndexHdr fields at correct byte offsets (iVersion at 0, szPage at 14, mxFrame at 16, etc.).
2. `test_wal_index_header_duplication` -- Write header copy 1, copy 2; verify reader accepts matching copies, rejects mismatched.
3. `test_wal_ckpt_info_layout` -- nBackfill at offset 96, aReadMark[5] at 100-119, aLock[8] at 120-127, nBackfillAttempted at 128.
4. `test_reader_marks_prevent_checkpoint_overwrite` -- Set reader mark to frame N; checkpoint MUST NOT overwrite frames <= N.
5. `test_lock_slot_mapping` -- aLock[0]=WRITE_LOCK, aLock[1]=CKPT_LOCK, aLock[2]=RECOVER_LOCK, aLock[3..7]=READ_LOCK(0..4).
6. `test_sqlite_master_schema` -- sqlite_master has exactly 5 columns: type, name, tbl_name, rootpage, sql.
7. `test_sqlite_master_page1_root` -- sqlite_master is always rooted at page 1.
8. `test_sqlite_master_types` -- Valid type values are 'table', 'index', 'view', 'trigger'.
9. `test_sqlite_master_auto_index_null_sql` -- Auto-created indexes have sql=NULL in sqlite_master.
10. `test_encoding_utf8_default` -- New database defaults to text encoding 1 (UTF-8).
11. `test_encoding_utf16le` -- Database with encoding=2 stores text as UTF-16LE; memcmp ordering differs from UTF-8.
12. `test_encoding_immutable` -- Attempting to change encoding after creation fails.
13. `test_binary_collation_memcmp` -- BINARY collation compares raw bytes via memcmp.
14. `test_nocase_collation_unicode` -- NOCASE collation operates on Unicode code points regardless of encoding.

## E2E TEST

Create a database in FrankenSQLite, populate with tables/indexes/views/triggers, verify sqlite_master contains correct rows. Open in C SQLite and verify all schema objects are visible. Test multi-process WAL-index sharing: one process writes, another reads via shared SHM; verify reader marks prevent stale reads.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA

- WAL-index header layout byte-for-byte compatible with C SQLite SHM format.
- Dual-copy header mechanism provides lock-free read consistency.
- Reader marks correctly prevent checkpoint from overwriting active reader frames.
- sqlite_master schema matches C SQLite exactly with all 5 columns.
- Text encoding set at creation and immutable; UTF-8/UTF-16le/UTF-16be all supported.
- Multi-process SHM interop with C SQLite verified.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T06:03:35.292588917Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T21:45:59.439620702Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T21:45:59.439584043Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-94us","depends_on_id":"bd-294","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-94us","depends_on_id":"bd-lldk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1673,"issue_id":"bd-94us","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## WAL Index (SHM) + sqlite_master Table + Encoding

### Spec Content (Lines 13979-14069, sections 11.10-11.12)

**11.10 WAL Index / wal-index / SHM (lines 13979-14035)**

**Byte order:** Unlike the main database file and WAL file (big-endian), all WAL-index (SHM) header fields are stored in **native byte order** of the host machine (except salt values copied verbatim from WAL header). SHM is not involved in crash recovery and does not need to be portable across architectures.

**Header (136 bytes):**
```
[0..48]:   WalIndexHdr (first copy):
             iVersion(u32) = 3007000 (MUST match)
             unused(u32)
             iChange(u32)
             isInit(u8)
             bigEndCksum(u8)
             szPage(u16)
             mxFrame(u32)
             nPage(u32)
             aFrameCksum[2](u32)
             aSalt[2](u32)
             aCksum[2](u32)

[48..96]:  WalIndexHdr (second copy)
           Lock-free reads: reader reads both copies, uses them only if they match.

[96..136]: WalCkptInfo (40 bytes total):
             nBackfill(u32) at offset 96
             aReadMark[5](u32) at offsets 100-119 (5 reader marks, 20 bytes)
             aLock[8](u8) at offsets 120-127 (8 SHM lock slots, 1 byte each)
             nBackfillAttempted(u32) at offset 128
             notUsed0(u32) at offset 132
```

**Hash table segments (32 KB each):**
- Physical layout: page-number array (`u32[4096]`) at bytes `[0..16384)` and hash table (`ht_slot[8192]`, u16) at bytes `[16384..32768)` in ALL segments.
- First segment: 136-byte header overlaps first 34 u32 page-number slots, leaving 4062 usable entries (wal.c compile-time assert).
  - `[0..136)`: Header
  - `[136..16384)`: Page number array: 4062 entries x 4 bytes
  - `[16384..32768)`: Hash table: 8192 slots x 2 bytes
- Subsequent segments: full 4096 entries (32 KB region).
  - `[0..16384)`: Page number array: 4096 entries x 4 bytes
  - `[16384..32768)`: Hash table: 8192 slots x 2 bytes

**Hash function:** `(page_number * 383) & 8191`, linear probing.
- NOT simple modulo. Prime multiplier 383 (`HASHTABLE_HASH_1` in C SQLite) provides better distribution for sequential page numbers.
- Using `page_number % 8192` would produce a working but INCOMPATIBLE wal-index when sharing SHM files with C SQLite in multi-process mode.

**Reader marks (offsets 100-119):** 5 reader marks (u32 each, 20 bytes total). Each records the WAL frame count at the time a reader began. Prevents checkpoint from overwriting frames still needed by active readers.

**WAL-index lock slot mapping (required for Hybrid SHM interop):**
- `aLock[0]` (byte 120) = `WAL_WRITE_LOCK` (exclusive; writer exclusion)
- `aLock[1]` (byte 121) = `WAL_CKPT_LOCK`
- `aLock[2]` (byte 122) = `WAL_RECOVER_LOCK`
- `aLock[3 + i]` (bytes 123..127) = `WAL_READ_LOCK(i)` for `i in 0..4`

These bytes are lockable file regions. Their values are not used as a coordination protocol; correctness depends on OS-level locks taken on these byte offsets.

**11.11 sqlite_master Table (lines 14037-14056)**

Every database contains a `sqlite_master` table (page 1 root) with this schema:
```sql
CREATE TABLE sqlite_master (
    type TEXT,      -- 'table', 'index', 'view', 'trigger'
    name TEXT,      -- object name
    tbl_name TEXT,  -- associated table name (for indexes/triggers)
    rootpage INT,   -- root B-tree page number (0 for views/triggers)
    sql TEXT        -- CREATE statement text (NULL for auto-indexes)
);
```

For the temp database, the equivalent is `sqlite_temp_master`.

On database creation, FrankenSQLite creates page 1 as a table leaf page containing zero rows in sqlite_master. First `CREATE TABLE` inserts a row with the CREATE statement text.

**11.12 Encoding (lines 14057-14069)**

**Default:** UTF-8 (text encoding = 1 at header offset 56).

**UTF-16 alternatives:** UTF-16le (2) and UTF-16be (3) supported. Encoding is set at database creation and cannot be changed afterward. When UTF-16 is used, all text in the database is UTF-16 encoded, and text comparisons use UTF-16 collation.

**Encoding affects comparison:**
- BINARY collation uses `memcmp` on raw bytes. For UTF-8, this produces correct Unicode code point ordering. For UTF-16, byte-order matters (LE vs BE).
- NOCASE collation always operates on Unicode code points regardless of encoding.

### Unit Tests Required

1. **test_wal_index_header_size**: Verify total header is 136 bytes (48 + 48 + 40).
2. **test_wal_index_hdr_copy_match**: Create WalIndexHdr, write both copies at offsets 0..48 and 48..96. Read both, verify they match.
3. **test_wal_index_hdr_iversion**: Verify iVersion field is 3007000.
4. **test_wal_index_native_byte_order**: On current platform, write header fields in native byte order. Read them back, verify they decode correctly without byte-swapping.
5. **test_wal_index_ckpt_info_layout**: Verify WalCkptInfo fields at correct offsets: nBackfill(96), aReadMark(100-119), aLock(120-127), nBackfillAttempted(128), notUsed0(132).
6. **test_wal_index_hash_function**: Verify `(page_number * 383) & 8191` for page numbers 1, 2, 100, 4096. Verify it is NOT `page_number % 8192`.
7. **test_wal_index_hash_distribution**: Hash 1000 sequential page numbers, verify distribution across 8192 slots is reasonably uniform (no excessive clustering).
8. **test_wal_index_hash_linear_probing**: Insert page numbers that hash to the same slot, verify linear probing finds the correct entries.
9. **test_wal_index_first_segment_capacity**: Verify first segment has 4062 usable entries (136 / 4 = 34 slots consumed by header; 4096 - 34 = 4062).
10. **test_wal_index_subsequent_segment_capacity**: Verify subsequent segments have 4096 entries.
11. **test_wal_index_segment_physical_layout**: Verify page-number array at bytes 0..16384 and hash table at bytes 16384..32768 in a 32KB segment.
12. **test_wal_index_reader_marks**: Set 5 reader marks at offsets 100-119 with different WAL frame counts. Read them back, verify correct values.
13. **test_wal_index_lock_slots**: Verify lock slot mapping: byte 120=WRITE, 121=CKPT, 122=RECOVER, 123-127=READ(0..4).
14. **test_sqlite_master_schema**: Verify the sqlite_master table has exactly 5 columns: type(TEXT), name(TEXT), tbl_name(TEXT), rootpage(INT), sql(TEXT).
15. **test_sqlite_master_type_values**: Verify valid type values are 'table', 'index', 'view', 'trigger'.
16. **test_sqlite_master_page1_root**: Verify sqlite_master is rooted on page 1.
17. **test_sqlite_master_empty_on_create**: Create a new database, verify sqlite_master on page 1 has zero rows (table leaf page with 0 cells).
18. **test_encoding_utf8_default**: Create database, verify header offset 56 = 1 (UTF-8).
19. **test_encoding_utf16le**: Create database with UTF-16le, verify header offset 56 = 2.
20. **test_encoding_binary_collation_utf8**: Verify BINARY collation on UTF-8 text produces correct Unicode code point ordering via memcmp.
21. **test_encoding_nocase_utf8_vs_utf16**: Verify NOCASE collation produces identical ordering results for the same strings regardless of UTF-8 vs UTF-16 encoding.

### E2E Tests

**test_e2e_wal_index_multi_reader**: Open a database in WAL mode, start 5 readers at different points (setting different reader marks). Verify reader marks prevent checkpoint from overwriting frames needed by active readers. After all readers close, verify checkpoint can proceed.

**test_e2e_sqlite_master_schema_tracking**: Create several tables, indexes, views, and a trigger. Read sqlite_master. Verify each object has a correct row with type, name, tbl_name, rootpage, and sql fields. Drop a table, verify its rows are removed from sqlite_master.

**test_e2e_wal_index_hash_lookup**: Write 5000 frames to WAL (spanning multiple hash segments). Look up various page numbers via the hash table. Verify all lookups return the correct latest frame for each page.

**test_e2e_encoding_utf16_database**: Create a database with UTF-16le encoding, insert text with international characters, read it back and verify the data is stored as UTF-16le in the database file and comparisons work correctly.
","created_at":"2026-02-08T06:30:20Z"},{"id":1674,"issue_id":"bd-94us","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Logging Requirements

- DEBUG: wal-index operations (insert/lookup) with probe lengths.
- INFO: sqlite_master load summary: `tables`, `indexes`, `views`, `triggers`.
","created_at":"2026-02-08T07:42:24Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-966ja","title":"G2.1: fault matrix for batched append and publish paths","description":"## Goal
Build the authoritative fault matrix for batched append and publish paths so every later WAL batching, publication, and recovery bead is grounded in an explicit failure model rather than scattered intuition.

## Why This Matters
The new parallel WAL program changes where ordering, durability, and visibility truth are established. That means append, publish, checkpoint, reset, and restart failure points must be enumerated before implementation and proof beads can claim correctness. This bead is the canonical catalog of those failure points and the expected observable outcomes.

## Scope
Cover fault points and expected outcomes for at least:
- local frame staging before durable publication,
- ordered durability or combiner entry,
- commit-certificate assignment or equivalent ordering record publication,
- visibility metadata publication,
- checkpoint overlap,
- truncate or reset,
- restart and recovery after abnormal termination,
- stale-lane cleanup and replay continuity.

For each case, define:
- injected fault point,
- preconditions,
- expected durability and visibility outcome,
- allowed fallback or safe-mode behavior,
- required logs and artifact fields,
- verification surface that exercises the case.

## Required Verification and Logging
- Deterministic fault-table tests for the matrix rows themselves.
- Integration or fault-injection scenarios that exercise append, publication, checkpoint, reset, and restart transitions.
- A named operator entrypoint such as `scripts/verify_g2_1_fault_matrix.sh` that replays the matrix and writes artifact manifests.
- Structured logs exposing at minimum `trace_id`, `scenario_id`, `fault_point`, `wal_lane_id`, `commit_certificate`, `publication_generation`, `expected_outcome`, `observed_outcome`, and first-failure diagnostics.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The matrix covers the append-to-publication path comprehensively enough that later proof beads can reference it mechanically.
- Every row names the required observable outcome, not just the injected fault.
- The matrix is reusable by recovery, checkpoint, and performance-proof beads.
- Logging and replay requirements are explicit enough that another agent can diagnose a failure without reopening this design debate.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The bead defines a replayable fault matrix spanning append staging, ordered durability, publication, checkpoint overlap, reset, restart, and stale-lane cleanup.
2. Each matrix row names preconditions, injected fault point, expected durability and visibility outcome, allowed fallback, and required evidence.
3. `scripts/verify_g2_1_fault_matrix.sh` or an equivalent named entrypoint is specified for replaying the matrix and collecting artifacts.
4. Structured logs expose fault point, lane identity, commit certificate context, publication generation, expected versus observed outcome, and first-failure diagnostics.
5. The matrix is self-contained enough to serve as a prerequisite for later recovery and failure-injection proof beads.","notes":"Program intent:
- this bead is the canonical failure-model companion to the parallel WAL work
- later WAL proof beads should cite this matrix rather than inventing ad hoc fault cases","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-22T03:34:40.082003659Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-06T17:16:16.156025441Z","closed_at":"2026-04-06T17:16:16.155795150Z","close_reason":"Documented WAL append/publish fault matrix in fsqlite-wal lib docs","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["db300-program","db300-track-g","fault-injection","logging","verification","wal"]}
 {"id":"bd-98io2","title":"V3.1: JIT template — closure compiler for simple sequential INSERT","description":"## What
Implement the first JIT template: compile `INSERT INTO t(cols...) VALUES (?1, ?2, ...)` with autoincrement rowid to a native Rust closure that bypasses the VDBE interpreter.

## Pattern to match
The opcode sequence for a simple prepared INSERT:
```
Init → Transaction → OpenWrite(cursor, root, N) → [SetSnapshot] →
NewRowid(cursor, r_rowid) → [Variable ?1 → r1, Variable ?2 → r2, ...] →
MakeRecord(r_start, N, r_record) → Insert(cursor, r_record, r_rowid) →
[IdxInsert for each index] → Close → Halt
```

## Generated closure signature
```rust
fn compiled_insert(
    txn: &RefCell<TransactionKind>,
    root_page: PageNumber,
    page_size: PageSize,
    params: &[SqliteValue],
    last_alloc_rowid: &mut i64,
    autoincrement_max: i64,
) -> Result<(usize, i64)>  // (affected_rows, last_insert_rowid)
```

## What the closure does
1. Allocate rowid: `last_alloc_rowid.max(autoincrement_max) + 1`
2. Serialize record: call `serialize_record_iter_into` directly from params slice
3. Open/reuse BtCursor on root_page (cursor can be a closure capture)
4. `table_insert_prechecked_absent` with append mode
5. Return (1, new_rowid)

No register file, no opcode dispatch, no bounds checking, no metrics branches.

## Files
- `crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/jit.rs` (NEW) — template matcher + closure generator
- `crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/engine.rs` — wire into maybe_trigger_jit
- `crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs` — dispatch to closure when available

## Estimated impact
~1µs dispatch overhead eliminated per row.
At 10K rows: ~10ms saved.

## Verification  
- Unit test: compile and execute a simple INSERT via closure, compare output to interpreter
- Conformance: no regressions
- Benchmark: section 1 tiny_1col improvement

+{"id":"bd-9e3xf","title":"PERF-btree: classify cell-slot cache misses (cold / invalidated / slot)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-24T05:44:25.330428110Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-24T05:44:35.432745682Z","closed_at":"2026-04-24T05:44:35.431461467Z","close_reason":"Split CELL_SLOT_CACHE_MISSES into cold/invalidated/slot. Audit (MemPageStore, 10k rows): delete_all_after_append = 9998/10098 (99%) MISS_INVALIDATED (writer-caused mutation_counter bump, not fixable without changing key). index_random_insert = 54739/66852 (82%) MISS_SLOT (binary-search probes unseen cell_idx on a correctly-cached page). CellRef::parse self-time = compute floor (~7.6ns); no branch-level specialisation paid (bd-o92pn.2 wash). Further optimisation needs (a) per-page write-epoch invariant under append-only writes or (b) skip-cache for delete-rebalance callers. This commit ships the classification infra.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-9iu50","title":"Track L: Pointer Swizzling — Eliminate Buffer Manager Hash Lookup Entirely","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-03-31T19:05:02.846035674Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-31T19:57:47.186628984Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-9iu50","depends_on_id":"bd-bj1rm","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-31T19:51:17.094934824Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-9iu50","depends_on_id":"bd-eorms","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-31T19:05:03.457789380Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":3382,"issue_id":"bd-9iu50","author":"ubuntu","text":"## Problem (from alien-graveyard §15.3)
Even with a flat page array (Track F), we still do pointer indirection: load page number → index into array → get page data. C SQLite stores raw memory pointers directly in B-tree node child slots. We should do the same.

## Solution: LeanStore-Style Pointer Swizzling
Tag the high bit of every page pointer: bit63=1 means "this is a raw memory address", bit63=0 means "this is a page ID needing lookup." On first access, CAS the parent's child pointer from page-ID to raw memory pointer. ALL subsequent accesses are direct memory dereferences — zero indirection, zero lookup, zero atomic operations.

## How It Works
1. B-tree node stores child pointers as u64
2. First access: load page via pager, get memory address
3. CAS parent's child slot: page_id → (memory_addr | 0x8000_0000_0000_0000)
4. Next access: check high bit → already swizzled → direct dereference
5. On eviction: un-swizzle by CAS back to page_id

## Alien Graveyard Reference: §15.3 (LeanStore), also used by Umbra DB

## Expected Impact: -0.08 to -0.15 µs per B-tree level traversed
For a 3-level tree: -0.24 to -0.45 µs total — this ALONE nearly closes the gap.

## Why This Is Different From Track F
Track F replaces DashMap with flat array (still indexed by page number).
Track L eliminates the index lookup entirely — parent nodes store raw pointers to child pages.
They compose: F provides the backing array, L makes traversal zero-cost.
","created_at":"2026-03-31T19:05:03Z"},{"id":3400,"issue_id":"bd-9iu50","author":"ubuntu","text":"## SAFETY: Pointer Swizzling Requires Controlled Unsafe
Pointer swizzling inherently requires dereferencing raw pointers (the swizzled memory address). This means:
1. The swizzle/un-swizzle code MUST live in fsqlite-vfs or fsqlite-pager (crates that already override workspace unsafe policy for mmap/shm)
2. The B-tree cursor code in fsqlite-btree must NOT contain unsafe — it calls a safe abstraction that handles the swizzled pointer internally
3. The safe abstraction: `fn read_page(&self, pgno_or_ptr: u64) -> &PageData` checks the tag bit and either indexes the flat array or dereferences the swizzled pointer — all within one unsafe block in the pager crate
4. ALL unsafe blocks must have SAFETY comments explaining the invariant (pointer is valid because page is epoch-pinned)

## Alternative: If the unsafe surface is too risky, implement pointer swizzling as a page-number-to-index cache instead (safe, ~80% of the benefit):
- Cache: HashMap<PageNumber, usize> where usize is the index into the flat page array
- Lookup: check cache first (2 ns), then fall back to pager (50 ns)
- This avoids ALL raw pointer manipulation while still eliminating most of the lookup cost
","created_at":"2026-03-31T19:42:04Z"}]}

### Created
- `sample_sqlite_db_files/FIXTURES.md` — comprehensive fixture ingestion guide

### Content Covers All Requirements
1. **Why fixtures are local-only**: .gitignore blocks *.db files, only metadata/checksums tracked
2. **How to ingest from /dp**: Step-by-step with `.backup` API (not `cp`), integrity verification
3. **Golden vs work copies**: Table comparing purpose, lifetime, mutability, git tracking
4. **SHA-256 provenance**: Three guarantees (immutability, reproducibility, auditability) + harness verification flow
5. **Add/remove fixtures**: Full procedures including checksum regeneration and metadata cleanup

### Additional Sections
- Safety policy (4 rules)
- Directory layout diagram
- Quick Start (4-step ingest + smoke test)
- PII/security considerations
- Re-verification script

### Acceptance Criterion Met
"A new contributor can ingest fixtures and run a smoke demo without tribal knowledge" — the Quick Start section provides exact commands from ingest through smoke test.

### Files Modified
- `sample_sqlite_db_files/FIXTURES.md` (created, ~200 lines)
- `sample_sqlite_db_files/.gitignore` (added `!FIXTURES.md` to tracked exceptions)
